title: Bignonia neoheterophylla
taxonomy: Bignonia neoheterophylla is classified as a species within the family Bignoniaceae, commonly known as the Bignonia or Trumpet Creeper family. This family is a part of the order Lamiales, which comprises flowering plants that have bilateral symmetry and have four- or five-merous flowers. The Bignoniaceae family is further divided into several genera, with Bignonia being one of the most well-known. Bignonia neoheterophylla is also classified as a member of the subfamily Bignonioideae, which contains around 1,500 species. The classification of Bignonia neoheterophylla reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history within the plant kingdom.

intro: Bignonia neoheterophylla, also known as "Coral Honeysuckle," is a woody vine native to Central and South America, belonging to the family Bignoniaceae. The health benefits of Bignonia neoheterophylla include its potential to lower blood pressure, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antioxidant properties, making it a valuable plant for cardiovascular health and anti-aging applications. The bioactive constituents of Bignonia neoheterophylla include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, such as heterophylline, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Bignonia neoheterophylla include teas, infusions, and extracts, which are used to treat various health conditions, including hypertension and diabetes.
